Last updated: 2026-04-13

# Cyoda — Complete Site Content

## What is Cyoda?

Cyoda is an open-source entity database management system (EDBMS) and workflow runtime. You define your entity types — the fields they carry, the states they can be in, and the valid transitions between states. Cyoda enforces those rules at the API boundary, records every state change immutably, and lets you query the history of any entity at any point in time.

It runs as a single binary with no distributed cluster required.

Cyoda supports two storage modes:

- **In-memory mode**: Stores entity state in process memory. Zero external dependencies. Starts in under 2 seconds. Resets when the process stops. Use for development and fast test cycles.
- **PostgreSQL mode**: Stores entity state durably in a PostgreSQL database. Persists across restarts. Use for integration testing and local validation with real persistence.

## What is an EDBMS?

An entity database management system (EDBMS) is a runtime that manages the full lifecycle of typed, stateful business objects — entities. Where a standard database stores rows, an EDBMS tracks state transitions, enforces lifecycle rules, and maintains a temporal audit trail. Cyoda adds a workflow engine to this model: transition rules are explicit, invalid transitions are rejected at the API boundary, and the complete state history is queryable.

## Key Capabilities

- **Stateful entities**: Define entity types with typed fields, valid states, and transition rules. Cyoda enforces the model at runtime.
- **Lifecycle enforcement**: Finite state machine engine. Invalid transitions are rejected at the API boundary — no defensive code required in your application.
- **Temporal history**: Every state change is recorded. Query the exact state of any entity at any point in time.
- **Immutable audit trail**: Non-modifiable record of all transitions: what changed, from which state, to which state, and when.
- **In-memory mode**: Zero external dependencies. Starts in under 2 seconds. Resets cleanly between test runs.
- **PostgreSQL mode**: Durable, transactional entity storage. State persists across restarts for local integration testing.
- **gRPC API**: Language-agnostic API surface. Client libraries for Java, Python, Go, and Kotlin. Any gRPC-capable language works.
- **Test harness**: Reset entity state between tests with a single API call. No teardown scripts, no manual cleanup.

## Supported Languages

Java, Python, Go, and Kotlin via gRPC client libraries. Any language with a gRPC implementation can connect to the Cyoda API directly.

## Three Ways to Use Cyoda

### Run it yourself
Download the binary and run Cyoda on your own machine or infrastructure. Local development, self-hosting, and experimentation. Free, Apache 2.0, no account required.
Download: https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go/releases

### Cyoda Cloud
The hosted SaaS option. Managed infrastructure, no cluster to operate. Get started immediately without installation.
URL: https://ai.cyoda.net/

### Enterprise Cyoda
Larger-scale infrastructure with enterprise support, SLA, and dedicated engagement for organisations with advanced operational requirements.
URL: https://cyoda.com

## Install and Run (Run it yourself)

Download the latest release from GitHub:
https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go/releases

### In-Memory Mode (recommended for getting started)
```bash
cyoda start --mode=memory
# HTTP API:  http://localhost:8080
# gRPC:      localhost:9090
```

### PostgreSQL Mode
```bash
cyoda start --mode=postgres \
  --postgres-url=postgres://localhost:5432/cyoda
```

### Docker
```bash
docker run -p 8080:8080 -p 9090:9090 \
  ghcr.io/cyoda-platform/cyoda:latest \
  --mode=memory
```

## Hello World Example (Go)

The example below creates a Greeting entity in the PENDING state, then drives a lifecycle transition based on the hour of day. Import paths are illustrative — see docs.cyoda.net for the published client libraries.

```go
package main

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "time"
    cyoda "github.com/cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go/client"
)

func main() {
    client, _ := cyoda.NewClient("localhost:9090")
    ctx := context.Background()

    entity, _ := client.CreateEntity(ctx, &cyoda.Entity{
        Type:  "Greeting",
        State: "PENDING",
        Data:  map[string]any{"hour": time.Now().Hour()},
    })

    if time.Now().Hour() < 12 {
        client.Transition(ctx, entity.ID, "TO_MORNING")
        fmt.Println("Good morning")
    } else {
        client.Transition(ctx, entity.ID, "TO_AFTERNOON")
        fmt.Println("Good afternoon")
    }
}
```

## FAQ

**What is Cyoda?**
Cyoda is an open-source entity database management system (EDBMS) and workflow runtime. You define your entity types, the valid states each entity can be in, and the transition rules between states. Cyoda enforces those rules, records every state change, and lets you query the history of any entity at any point in time. It runs as a single binary with no distributed cluster required.

**What is an EDBMS?**
An entity database management system (EDBMS) is a runtime that manages the full lifecycle of typed, stateful business objects — entities. Where a standard database stores rows, an EDBMS tracks state transitions, enforces lifecycle rules, and maintains a temporal audit trail. Cyoda adds a workflow engine to this model: transition rules are explicit, invalid transitions are rejected at the API boundary, and the complete state history is queryable.

**Why use stateful entities?**
Most backend systems need to track the state of business objects over time. Without a structured model, state logic ends up scattered across application code and database columns with no enforcement and no history. Cyoda gives each entity a defined lifecycle with enforced rules. Your application code drives transitions; Cyoda ensures they are valid and records them.

**What kinds of systems fit Cyoda?**
Systems where entities change state over time and where the history of those changes matters: workflow automation, order management, approval pipelines, event-driven backends, audit-sensitive applications, and anywhere you would otherwise build a hand-rolled state machine with an audit log.

**What is the difference between in-memory and PostgreSQL mode?**
In-memory mode stores entity state in process memory. It has zero external dependencies and resets when the process stops — useful for development and fast test cycles. PostgreSQL mode stores entity state durably in a PostgreSQL database, persisting across restarts.

**What is the difference between Run it yourself, Cyoda Cloud, and Enterprise Cyoda?**
Run it yourself: download the open-source binary and run Cyoda on your own machine or infrastructure. Free, Apache 2.0, no account required. Cyoda Cloud (ai.cyoda.net): the hosted SaaS option — managed infrastructure, no installation required. Enterprise Cyoda (cyoda.com): larger-scale deployment with enterprise support, SLA, and dedicated engagement.

**What languages are supported?**
Java, Python, Go, and Kotlin via gRPC client libraries. Any language with a gRPC implementation can connect to the Cyoda API directly.

**Where is the GitHub repo?**
https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go

**Where is the documentation?**
Full documentation is at docs.cyoda.net. The Cyoda quickstart guide covers installation, entity definition, and your first workflow in under ten minutes.

## Links

- GitHub: https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go
- Releases: https://github.com/Cyoda-platform/cyoda-light-go/releases
- Documentation: https://docs.cyoda.net
- Quick Start: https://docs.cyoda.net/cyodalight/quickstart
- Cyoda Cloud: https://ai.cyoda.net/
- Enterprise Cyoda: https://cyoda.com
